definition: Form as the material or visible side of experience, especially in aggregate analysis and sense-field contexts.
notes: The project keeps form as the default because it works across the aggregate framework and the sixfold sense-field system without forcing a single modern theory of matter. Material form is useful when the physical side needs to be made explicit, but body is too narrow and matter is too reified for blanket use. In sense-field contexts rūpā often means visible forms, while in the five heaps rūpa marks the material side of the analysis more broadly. Translators should keep the aggregate, visible-object, and nāmarūpa uses distinct and should not let matter or body become automatic replacements.

context_rules.context: In most aggregate and doctrinal contexts
When the physical side of the contrast with nāma needs to be explicit
context_rules.notes: Use in explanatory prose or where plain form would be too thin.

context_rules.context: In eye-and-form sense-field contexts such as cakkhuñca paṭicca rūpe ca
context_rules.notes: Use the visual-object sense rather than the broader aggregate sense.
Keep continuity with the house treatment of khandha.

example_phrases.pali: cakkhuñca paṭicca rūpe ca uppajjati cakkhuviññāṇaṃ
example_phrases.translation: dependent on eye and visible forms, eye-knowing arises
with name-and-form as condition, the six sense fields
example_phrases.notes: Shows rūpa inside the dependent-arising compound.

version_notes: Strengthened to distinguish aggregate, visible-object, and name-and-form uses without allowing body or matter to drift in as blanket defaults.
